headlineSummary: OpenAI and Microsoft Copilot drew similarly negative reaction with a close tie and limited evidence behind both, while Anthropic was more mixed thanks to one strong positive user experience offset by criticism [E1][E6].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot leaned negative on privacy concerns, with comments calling the default training-data setting shady and warning that sensitive files may be sent to Microsoft by default [E6][E7][E8].
OpenAI also leaned negative, driven by comments about Sora’s shutdown, trust concerns, and doubts about cash and moat strength [E1][E2][E3].
Anthropic looked mixed to slightly positive overall: one comment praised Claude Code for working well after little effort, but another criticized its bug handling and unresolved issues [E4][E5].

annotations.rationale: The title signals Sora is ending or being shut down, which is a negative release outcome for OpenAI's Sora product.

evidence.excerpt: I prefer to see it when someone like Google does it? Because they're really doing it from the standpoint of this has broad applicable applications to something like simulation or training. Not whatever open AI was doing which honestly just doesn't feel very truthful. I feel like they say one thing and do something else or they say one thing and the agenda or something else.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly criticizes OpenAI as untruthful and inconsistent about its intentions.

evidence.excerpt: Not good, seems like they are running out of cash and partners abandoning them. They had no real moat to be fair. Anthropic eating their lunch in enterprise and other players have cashflows from other businesses (XAI, Google)

annotations.rationale: In the OpenAI/Sora story context, "they" refers to OpenAI and the comment sharply criticizes its cash position and lack of moat.
"Anthropic eating their lunch in enterprise" is a strong favorable comparison for Anthropic.

evidence.excerpt: My favorite is the Claude Code bugtracker, on GitHub of course: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues There is some bot that will match your issue to some other 3 vaguely related issue, then auto close in 3 days. The other vaguely related issues are auto closed for inactivity. Nothing is ever fixed, which is why they can't keep the thing from messing with your scroll position for years now.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names Claude Code and strongly criticizes its bug handling and unresolved product issues.

evidence.excerpt: I used Claude code to build a custom notes application for my specific requirements. It’s not perfect, but I barely invested 10 hours in it and it does almost everything I could have asked for, plus some really cool stuff that mostly just works after one iteration.

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports a clearly positive experience using Claude Code, despite a minor caveat that it is not perfect.

evidence.excerpt: If you scroll down to "Allow GitHub to use my data for AI model training" in GitHub settings, you can enable or disable it. However, what really gets me is how they pitch it like it’s some kind of user-facing feature: Enabled = You will have access to the feature Disabled = You won't have access to the feature As if handing over your data for free is a perk. Kinda hilarious.

annotations.rationale: In the GitHub Copilot policy story context, the commenter criticizes framing data sharing for model training as a perk.

evidence.excerpt: "Allow GitHub to use my data for AI model training" is enabled by default. Turn it off here: https://github.com/settings/copilot/features Do they have this set on business accounts also by default? If so, this is really shady.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly points to Copilot settings and calls the default training-data opt-in "really shady."

evidence.excerpt: Fun fact: Copilot gives you no way to ignore sensitive files with API keys, passwords, DB credentials, etc.: https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/11254#discussi... So by default you send all this to Microsoft by opening your IDE.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly accuses Copilot of exposing sensitive files to Microsoft by default, a strong privacy criticism.
